📖 INTRODUCTION
Geometry is the study of shapes, sizes and positions. In this chapter we learn the basic building blocks of geometry — points, lines, angles and curves.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Point
→ A point is an exact location in space
→ It has no length, breadth or thickness
→ Represented by a dot and named by capital letter
→ Example: Point A, Point B

2. Line
→ A line extends infinitely in both directions
→ Has no endpoints
→ Named by any two points on it: line AB
→ Infinite points lie on a line

3. Line Segment
→ Part of a line with TWO fixed endpoints
→ Has definite length that can be measured
→ Named by its endpoints: segment AB

4. Ray
→ Starts at one endpoint and extends infinitely in ONE direction
→ Has ONE endpoint (starting point)
→ Example: ray of sunlight

5. Angle
→ Formed by TWO rays with same starting point (vertex)
→ Measured in degrees
→ Types: acute(<90°), right(90°), obtuse(90°-180°)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Angle = amount of rotation from one ray to another
→ Full angle = 360°
→ Straight angle = 180°
→ Right angle = 90°
→ Diameter = 2 × Radius
→ Number of diagonals in polygon = n(n-3)/2

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Corner of your notebook = right angle (90°)
Example 2: Railway tracks = parallel lines
Example 3: Clock hands = rays from center (vertex)
Example 4: Wheel = circle with center

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. How many diagonals in pentagon?
Formula: n(n-3)/2 = 5(5-3)/2 = 5×2/2 = 5 diagonals ✅

Q2. Name the parts: in ray PQ
Endpoint = P (starting point)
Direction = towards Q and beyond ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Point → no dimension
→ Line → 1 dimension, no endpoints
→ Line segment → 1 dimension, 2 endpoints
→ Ray → 1 dimension, 1 endpoint
→ Through 1 point → infinite lines possible
→ Through 2 points → exactly 1 line possible
→ Parallel lines → never meet
→ Intersecting lines → meet at one point

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
In this chapter we learn to measure and classify different shapes. We use tools like ruler, protractor and compass to understand shapes better.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Types of Angles
→ Acute angle: 0° to 90° (less than right angle)
→ Right angle: exactly 90°
→ Obtuse angle: 90° to 180°
→ Straight angle: exactly 180°
→ Reflex angle: 180° to 360°
→ Complete angle: exactly 360°

2. Types of Triangles (by sides)
→ Equilateral: all 3 sides equal, all angles 60°
→ Isosceles: 2 sides equal, 2 angles equal
→ Scalene: no sides equal, no angles equal

3. Types of Triangles (by angles)
→ Acute triangle: all angles < 90°
→ Right triangle: one angle = 90°
→ Obtuse triangle: one angle > 90°

4. Types of Quadrilaterals
→ Square: all sides equal, all angles 90°
→ Rectangle: opposite sides equal, all angles 90°
→ Rhombus: all sides equal, opposite angles equal
→ Parallelogram: opposite sides parallel and equal
→ Trapezium: one pair of parallel sides

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Sum of angles of triangle = 180°
→ Sum of angles of quadrilateral = 360°
→ Sum of angles of polygon = (n-2) × 180°
→ 1 complete revolution = 360°
→ 1 straight angle = 180° = 2 right angles
→ 1 right angle = 90°

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Slice of pizza = triangle
Example 2: Door = rectangle
Example 3: Tile on floor = square
Example 4: Clock at 3 o'clock = right angle

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. Two angles of triangle = 60° and 70°. Find third.
Third angle = 180° - 60° - 70° = 50° ✅

Q2. Classify triangle with sides 5cm, 5cm, 8cm.
Two sides equal → Isosceles triangle ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Triangle cannot have 2 obtuse angles
→ Equilateral triangle: all angles = 60°
→ Right triangle: longest side is hypotenuse
→ Square is a special rectangle
→ Rectangle is a special parallelogram
→ Rhombus is a special parallelogram

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Integers extend the number system beyond whole numbers by including negative numbers. They help us represent situations like temperature below zero, floors below ground, and money owed.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. What are Integers?
→ Integers = {...-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3...}
→ Include: negative numbers, zero, positive numbers
→ Natural numbers ⊂ Whole numbers ⊂ Integers

2. Number Line
→ Negative integers: LEFT of zero
→ Positive integers: RIGHT of zero
→ Moving right = increasing
→ Moving left = decreasing

3. Comparing Integers
→ Positive > Zero > Negative
→ Among negatives: closer to zero = greater
→ Example: -2 > -5 (because -2 is closer to 0)

4. Addition of Integers
→ Same signs: add and keep sign
→ Different signs: subtract and take sign of larger
→ Example: (-3)+(-4) = -7
→ Example: (-3)+(+7) = +4

5. Subtraction of Integers
→ a - b = a + (-b)
→ Subtracting negative = adding positive
→ Example: 5-(-3) = 5+3 = 8

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Additive inverse of n = -n
→ |n| = absolute value (always positive)
→ a - (-b) = a + b
→ (-a) + (-b) = -(a+b)
→ Sum of integer and its additive inverse = 0

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Temperature -5°C means 5 degrees below zero
Example 2: Basement B2 = floor -2
Example 3: Loss of Rs.50 = -50 in accounts
Example 4: 200m below sea level = -200m

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. Find: (-8) + (+5)
|-8|=8, |+5|=5. Difference=3. Larger is negative.
Answer = -3 ✅

Q2. Find: 7 - (-4)
= 7 + 4 = 11 ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ 0 is neither positive nor negative
→ Negative numbers go left on number line
→ -1 > -100 (closer to zero is greater)
→ Additive inverse of 0 is 0
→ |negative number| = positive number
→ Subtracting = adding opposite

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Fractions represent parts of a whole. When we cut a pizza into equal slices, each slice is a fraction of the whole pizza. Fractions are everywhere in daily life!

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Parts of a Fraction
→ Numerator = top number (parts we have)
→ Denominator = bottom number (total equal parts)
→ Example: 3/5 means 3 parts out of 5 equal parts

2. Types of Fractions
→ Proper fraction: numerator < denominator (3/5)
→ Improper fraction: numerator ≥ denominator (7/3)
→ Mixed fraction: whole number + proper fraction (2⅓)
→ Unit fraction: numerator = 1 (1/4, 1/7)

3. Equivalent Fractions
→ Same value, different appearance
→ 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8
→ Multiply/divide both parts by same number

4. Comparing Fractions
→ Same denominator: compare numerators
→ Different denominator: convert to same denominator using LCM

5. Operations
→ Add/Subtract: make denominators same first
→ Multiply: multiply numerators, multiply denominators
→ Divide: multiply by reciprocal

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Equivalent fraction: a/b = (a×n)/(b×n)
→ Simplest form: divide by HCF of numerator and denominator
→ a/b + c/b = (a+c)/b (same denominator)
→ a/b × c/d = (a×c)/(b×d)
→ a/b ÷ c/d = a/b × d/c (multiply by reciprocal)
→ Mixed to improper: (whole×denominator + numerator)/denominator

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: 1/2 hour = 30 minutes
Example 2: 3/4 of Rs.100 = Rs.75
Example 3: Pizza cut in 8 slices, eat 3 = 3/8 eaten
Example 4: 2/5 of class absent = 2 out of every 5 students

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. Simplify 12/18.
HCF(12,18) = 6. 12/18 = 2/3 ✅

Q2. Add 1/4 + 1/3.
LCM(4,3) = 12. 3/12 + 4/12 = 7/12 ✅

Q3. Convert 2¾ to improper fraction.
(2×4 + 3)/4 = 11/4 ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Denominator can NEVER be zero
→ Every whole number n = n/1
→ 1/2 > 1/3 > 1/4 (larger denominator = smaller fraction)
→ Reciprocal of a/b = b/a
→ Any number × its reciprocal = 1
→ Simplest form when HCF of numerator and denominator = 1

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Decimals are another way to write fractions with denominators 10, 100, 1000 etc. They make calculations easier especially in money, measurement and science!

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Place Value in Decimals
→ 4 5 3 . 7 8 9
→ 4 = hundreds, 5 = tens, 3 = ones
→ 7 = tenths (1/10)
→ 8 = hundredths (1/100)
→ 9 = thousandths (1/1000)

2. Reading Decimals
→ 3.5 = three point five
→ 0.75 = zero point seven five
→ 12.08 = twelve point zero eight

3. Comparing Decimals
→ Compare whole number part first
→ Then compare digit by digit after decimal
→ Add zeros to make equal decimal places
→ Example: 0.5 = 0.50 (same value)

4. Operations with Decimals
→ Add/Subtract: align decimal points
→ Multiply: multiply normally, count decimal places
→ Divide by 10: move decimal 1 place LEFT
→ Multiply by 10: move decimal 1 place RIGHT

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ 1/10 = 0.1, 1/100 = 0.01, 1/1000 = 0.001
→ ×10 = move decimal 1 right
→ ×100 = move decimal 2 right
→ ÷10 = move decimal 1 left
→ ÷100 = move decimal 2 left
→ 1 cm = 0.01 m, 1 mm = 0.001 m
→ 1 g = 0.001 kg, 1 ml = 0.001 L

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Rs. 12.50 = twelve rupees fifty paise
Example 2: 1.5 kg = one and half kilogram
Example 3: 100m race in 9.58 seconds
Example 4: Petrol Rs. 96.72 per litre

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. 3.4 + 2.85 = ?
  3.40
+ 2.85
------
  6.25 ✅

Q2. 0.5 × 0.4 = ?
5 × 4 = 20. Total decimal places = 2.
Answer = 0.20 = 0.2 ✅

Q3. Convert 2.75 to fraction.
2.75 = 275/100 = 11/4 = 2¾ ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ 0.1 = 1/10, 0.01 = 1/100, 0.001 = 1/1000
→ Adding zeros after last decimal digit does not change value
→ 0.5 = 0.50 = 0.500 (all same)
→ Align decimal points before adding/subtracting
→ Count total decimal places when multiplying
→ 1/4 = 0.25, 1/2 = 0.5, 3/4 = 0.75

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Data handling means collecting, organizing and understanding information. We use graphs and averages to make sense of data. This helps in making good decisions!

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Data Collection
→ Data = collection of facts and numbers
→ Raw data = unorganized collected information
→ Organized data = arranged in order or table

2. Tally Marks
→ Used for quick counting
→ 4 vertical marks + 1 diagonal = 5
→ Makes counting in groups of 5 easy

3. Types of Graphs
→ Pictograph: uses pictures/symbols
→ Bar graph: uses rectangular bars
→ Both have a key/scale

4. Measures of Central Tendency
→ Mean (Average) = Sum ÷ Count
→ Median = middle value when arranged in order
→ Mode = most frequently occurring value
→ Range = Maximum - Minimum

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Mean = Sum of all values / Number of values
→ Range = Largest value - Smallest value
→ Median = middle value (odd count)
→ Median = average of two middle values (even count)
→ Mode = value appearing most often
→ Sum = Mean × Count

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Average marks of class = Mean
Example 2: Most popular sport in school = Mode
Example 3: Middle student's height = Median
Example 4: Temperature range in week = Range

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. Find mean of 4, 8, 6, 2, 10.
Sum = 4+8+6+2+10 = 30. Count = 5.
Mean = 30/5 = 6 ✅

Q2. Find median of 3, 7, 2, 9, 5.
Arrange: 2, 3, 5, 7, 9. Middle = 5.
Median = 5 ✅

Q3. Find mode of 2, 3, 3, 4, 3, 2.
Count: 2→twice, 3→three times, 4→once.
Mode = 3 ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Mean uses all values in calculation
→ Median is not affected by extreme values
→ Mode is most popular value
→ A dataset can have no mode, one mode or many modes
→ Mean can be decimal even if all data is whole numbers
→ Always arrange data in order before finding median

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Mensuration is the branch of mathematics that deals with measurement of shapes — their perimeter (boundary length) and area (surface covered). It is used in construction, farming, and daily life!

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Perimeter
→ Total length of boundary of a shape
→ Measured in cm, m, km
→ Used for: fencing, framing, borders

2. Area
→ Amount of surface enclosed by a shape
→ Measured in sq cm, sq m, sq km
→ Used for: painting, carpeting, tiling

3. Perimeter Formulas
→ Square: P = 4 × side
→ Rectangle: P = 2 × (length + breadth)
→ Triangle: P = a + b + c (sum of all sides)
→ Regular polygon: P = n × side

4. Area Formulas
→ Square: A = side × side = side²
→ Rectangle: A = length × breadth

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Perimeter of square = 4s
→ Perimeter of rectangle = 2(l+b)
→ Area of square = s²
→ Area of rectangle = l × b
→ Side of square = Perimeter ÷ 4
→ Length = Area ÷ Breadth
→ 1 m = 100 cm
→ 1 sq m = 10,000 sq cm
→ 1 hectare = 10,000 sq m

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Fencing a garden = perimeter problem
Example 2: Carpeting a room = area problem
Example 3: Painting a wall = area problem
Example 4: Putting border on photo frame = perimeter

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. Room is 8m × 6m. Cost of flooring at Rs.50/sq m?
Area = 8 × 6 = 48 sq m.
Cost = 48 × 50 = Rs. 2400 ✅

Q2. Square garden has perimeter 40m. Find area.
Side = 40/4 = 10m.
Area = 10 × 10 = 100 sq m ✅

Q3. Rectangle has area 36 sq cm, length 9 cm. Find breadth.
Breadth = Area/Length = 36/9 = 4 cm ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Perimeter = boundary (1D measurement)
→ Area = surface (2D measurement)
→ Same perimeter can have different areas
→ Square has maximum area for given perimeter
→ Always use same units before calculating
→ 1 m² = 10,000 cm² (not 100!)

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Algebra uses letters to represent unknown numbers. This makes it possible to solve problems where the value is not known! Algebra is the language of mathematics used in science, engineering and computers.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Variables and Constants
→ Variable: letter representing unknown (x, y, n, a)
→ Constant: fixed number (3, -7, 100)
→ Coefficient: number multiplied with variable (in 5x, 5 is coefficient)

2. Algebraic Expressions
→ Combination of variables, constants and operations
→ Examples: 3x+5, 2y-7, 4a+3b
→ Terms: parts separated by + or - signs

3. Equations
→ Algebraic expression with equal sign
→ Left Hand Side (LHS) = Right Hand Side (RHS)
→ Solving: find value of variable that makes LHS = RHS

4. Solving Simple Equations
→ Keep variable on one side
→ Do same operation on both sides
→ Always check answer by substituting back

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Perimeter of square = 4s (s = side)
→ Perimeter of rectangle = 2(l+b)
→ Simple interest = P×R×T/100
→ Distance = Speed × Time
→ If LHS = RHS, equation is balanced
→ Adding same number to both sides keeps equation balanced

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Age problem — if age is x, after 5 years = x+5
Example 2: Cost — if pen costs p, 6 pens cost 6p
Example 3: Speed — distance = 60t (t = time in hours)
Example 4: Temperature — Celsius to Fahrenheit uses algebra

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. Solve: x + 9 = 15
x = 15 - 9 = 6. Check: 6+9=15 ✅

Q2. Solve: 3n = 24
n = 24/3 = 8. Check: 3×8=24 ✅

Q3. Solve: 2x - 4 = 10
2x = 10+4 = 14. x = 7.
Check: 2×7-4=14-4=10 ✅

Q4. A number doubled and added 5 gives 17. Find number.
2x + 5 = 17. 2x = 12. x = 6 ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Variable can take different values
→ Equation has exactly one solution (usually)
→ Same operation on both sides keeps balance
→ Check answer by substituting back
→ x + 0 = x (additive identity)
→ x × 1 = x (multiplicative identity)
→ Linear equation has variable with power 1 only

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Ratio compares two quantities of same kind. Proportion says two ratios are equal. These concepts are used in cooking, maps, mixing, sharing money and many real life situations!

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Ratio
→ Comparison of two quantities by division
→ Written as a:b or a/b
→ Always in same units
→ No units in ratio (just numbers)
→ Simplify by dividing by HCF

2. Proportion
→ When two ratios are equal: a:b = c:d
→ Written as a:b::c:d
→ Product of extremes = Product of means
→ a×d = b×c

3. Unitary Method
→ Find value of ONE unit first
→ Then multiply for required units
→ Example: if 5 items cost Rs.25, 1 item = Rs.5

4. Direct and Inverse Proportion
→ Direct: both increase or decrease together
→ Inverse: one increases, other decreases

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Ratio a:b = a/b (simplify using HCF)
→ Proportion: a:b::c:d means a×d = b×c
→ Unitary method: Unit value = Total/Count
→ Direct proportion: a/b = c/d
→ Inverse proportion: a×b = c×d
→ Dividing in ratio m:n → m/(m+n) and n/(m+n)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Recipe — 2 cups flour:1 cup sugar ratio = 2:1
Example 2: Map scale — 1:50000 means 1cm = 500m
Example 3: Sharing profit — in ratio 3:2 means 3 parts and 2 parts
Example 4: Speed — more speed, less time (inverse proportion)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. Simplify ratio 24:36.
HCF(24,36) = 12. 24:36 = 2:3 ✅

Q2. Divide Rs.500 in ratio 2:3.
Total parts=5. First = 2/5×500=Rs.200.
Second = 3/5×500=Rs.300 ✅

Q3. If 8 pens cost Rs.56, find cost of 5 pens.
1 pen = 56/8 = Rs.7. 5 pens = 5×7 = Rs.35 ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Ratio has no units
→ Always convert to same unit before finding ratio
→ In proportion: product of means = product of extremes
→ Unitary method: find 1 first, then multiply
→ More workers = less days (inverse proportion)
→ More speed = less time (inverse proportion)
→ More items = more cost (direct proportion)

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Symmetry means balance and proportion. A shape is symmetric when it looks the same after being folded, reflected or rotated. Symmetry is found everywhere in nature, art and architecture!

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Line of Symmetry
→ A line that divides figure into two mirror image halves
→ Also called axis of symmetry
→ When folded along this line, both halves overlap perfectly

2. Lines of Symmetry in Shapes
→ Equilateral triangle: 3 lines
→ Square: 4 lines
→ Rectangle: 2 lines
→ Circle: infinite lines
→ Isosceles triangle: 1 line
→ Scalene triangle: 0 lines
→ Regular polygon (n sides): n lines

3. Symmetry in Letters
→ Vertical symmetry: A, H, I, M, O, T, U, V, W, X, Y
→ Horizontal symmetry: B, C, D, E, H, I, K, O, X
→ Both: H, I, O, X
→ No symmetry: F, G, J, L, N, P, Q, R, S, Z

4. Reflection
→ Mirror image of a figure
→ Left becomes right, right becomes left
→ Size remains same, orientation flips

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS
→ Regular polygon with n sides → n lines of symmetry
→ Circle → infinite lines of symmetry
→ Equilateral triangle → 3 lines
→ Square → 4 lines (2 through midpoints + 2 diagonals)
→ Rectangle → 2 lines (only through midpoints, NOT diagonals)
→ Rhombus → 2 lines (only diagonals, NOT through midpoints)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Human face is (approximately) symmetric
Example 2: Butterfly wings are symmetric
Example 3: Taj Mahal is perfectly symmetric
Example 4: Snowflakes have 6-fold symmetry

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. How many lines of symmetry in regular pentagon?
Regular polygon with 5 sides → 5 lines of symmetry ✅

Q2. Does letter S have line symmetry?
No! S has no line of symmetry.
It has rotational symmetry (looks same after 180° rotation) ✅

Q3. Rectangle has how many lines of symmetry?
2 lines — one horizontal, one vertical.
Note: diagonals of rectangle are NOT lines of symmetry ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Symmetric = balanced appearance
→ Line of symmetry divides into EQUAL mirror halves
→ Square: 4 lines, Rectangle: 2 lines (important difference!)
→ Rhombus: 2 lines (diagonals only)
→ Regular hexagon: 6 lines of symmetry
→ Reflection flips left-right
→ Rotational symmetry: figure looks same after rotation

fields.title: Reading Lesson
fields.text_content:
📖 INTRODUCTION
Practical Geometry teaches us to draw accurate geometric figures using compass and ruler. These constructions are exact — no measurement errors! This skill is used in engineering, architecture and design.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔑 KEY CONCEPTS

1. Basic Tools
→ Compass: draws circles and arcs, copies lengths
→ Ruler (straightedge): draws straight lines, measures
→ Protractor: measures angles (not used in compass constructions)
→ Set square: draws 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° angles

2. Construction of Line Segment
→ Draw a ray
→ Set compass to required length
→ Mark arc on ray → line segment done

3. Perpendicular Bisector
→ Open compass more than half of segment
→ Draw arcs from both endpoints
→ Connect intersection points
→ Result: perpendicular line at midpoint (90°)

4. Angle Bisector
→ Draw arc from vertex cutting both arms
→ Draw equal arcs from intersection points
→ Join vertex to new intersection
→ Result: line dividing angle into two equal parts

5. Constructing Angles
→ 60°: equilateral triangle method
→ 90°: perpendicular bisector method
→ 30°: bisect 60°
→ 45°: bisect 90°
→ 120°: extend 60° construction
→ 75°: 60° + bisect 30°

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

➕ FORMULAS AND STEPS
→ Perpendicular bisector: compass > half segment, arcs from both ends
→ Angle bisector: arc from vertex, equal arcs from arms
→ 60°: equilateral triangle (all sides equal = all angles 60°)
→ 90° = 2 × 45° = perpendicular line
→ 30° = 60° ÷ 2
→ 45° = 90° ÷ 2
→ 120° = 180° - 60°
→ 75° = 45° + 30°
→ 135° = 90° + 45°

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

💡 REAL LIFE EXAMPLES

Example 1: Architects use geometric constructions for building plans
Example 2: Engineers use perpendicular lines for right angles in construction
Example 3: Artists use symmetry and geometric constructions in designs
Example 4: Map makers use scale constructions

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 SOLVED EXAMPLES

Q1. Steps to construct 60° angle:
1. Draw ray AB
2. With center A, draw arc cutting AB at P
3. With same radius, center P, draw arc cutting first arc at Q
4. Join AQ → angle QAB = 60° ✅

Q2. Steps for perpendicular bisector of AB:
1. Open compass more than half AB
2. Draw arcs from A (above and below)
3. Draw arcs from B with same opening
4. Connect intersection points P and Q
5. PQ is perpendicular bisector ✅

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

⭐ REMEMBER THIS!
→ Compass opening must be MORE than half for perpendicular bisector
→ 60° uses equilateral triangle principle
→ Bisecting = dividing into 2 equal parts
→ Perpendicular = meeting at 90°
→ Construction is more accurate than measurement
→ Same compass opening must be maintained throughout one construction
